title: SEGMENT 16 — Where pharmacogenomics is going: the integrated prescription

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Where pharmacogenomics is going: the integrated prescription
Let me bring all of this together and tell you where the field is heading over the next ten to twenty years.
The trajectory, in one sentence, is: pharmacogenomics is becoming a routine part of every prescription, invisibly, in the background.
Here's what that looks like in practice.

A patient walks into any clinical setting — primary care, urgent care, ER, specialty clinic — and their preemptive pharmacogenomics panel has been done, sometime in their past, and lives in their record.
Maybe it was done as part of a routine adult wellness visit.
Maybe it was done at birth as part of newborn screening, extended beyond the classic metabolic disease screening into pharmacogenomics.
Maybe it was done as part of a national population genomics program.
The mechanism doesn't matter.
The result is there.
When the clinician writes a prescription, the EHR checks the patient's relevant genotypes against the drug's pharmacogenomics profile, automatically, in milliseconds.
Most of the time, nothing happens — the drug doesn't have a clinically-significant pharmacogenomic interaction, or the patient's genotype doesn't require adjustment.
Occasionally, an alert fires — and the alert is intelligent, contextual, and actionable, not a generic warning the prescriber will dismiss without reading.
"This patient is a CYP2D6 ultrarapid metabolizer; the standard dose of this antidepressant may not achieve therapeutic concentrations; consider an alternative drug or a higher starting dose.
Click here for the CPIC guideline.
" The prescriber acts.
The right drug at the right dose goes out the door.
This vision requires several things to mature in parallel.
Sequencing has to be cheaper and more comprehensive.
We're already mostly there.
A whole-genome sequence, in 2026, costs a few hundred dollars.
The pharmacogenomically-relevant content can be extracted from any whole genome, so once a patient has a sequence done for any reason, pharmacogenomics is essentially free as a byproduct.
The cost trajectory continues downward.
EHR integration has to mature.
This is happening, slowly.
The major EHR vendors have rolled out pharmacogenomics decision-support modules.
Adoption is uneven.
The user experience is improving.
Standards bodies like HL7 FHIR are developing data exchange formats specifically for pharmacogenomics.
Polygenic dose prediction.
Right now, most pharmacogenomics dosing is single-gene or two-gene — warfarin uses CYP2C9 and VKORC1, clopidogrel uses CYP2C19.
But emerging research suggests that for some drugs, dose response is influenced by many genes at small effect sizes, in addition to the major-effect genes.
Polygenic risk scores for drug response — analogous to polygenic risk scores for disease — are an active research area.
The idea is to predict a patient's optimal dose by summing contributions across hundreds or thousands of genetic variants, not just the canonical pharmacogenes.
This is early days, but for some drugs it could materially improve prediction.
Pediatric and developmental pharmacogenomics.
A whole subfield is emerging around how pharmacogenomics interacts with developmental changes in enzyme expression.
Neonates and infants have immature CYP systems; the pharmacogenomic implications differ from adults.
The same poor metabolizer phenotype may have different consequences at different ages.
Pediatric guidelines are catching up to adult guidelines.
Pharmacogenomics for non-prescription products.
Many over-the-counter drugs, dietary supplements, and recreational drugs have pharmacogenomic profiles too.
Caffeine metabolism is largely controlled by CYP1A2 — fast metabolizers tolerate caffeine differently than slow metabolizers.
Alcohol metabolism is shaped by ADH and ALDH variants — the famous "Asian flush" reaction.
These extend pharmacogenomics from the prescription pad into daily life.
The endpoint is a world where prescribing without considering pharmacogenomics is as obviously inappropriate as prescribing without considering kidney function.
Where every patient's genotype-relevant profile is part of their basic medical information, alongside their allergies and their problem list.
Where adverse drug reactions of the kind that fill pharmacology textbooks become rare events, identifiable in advance, preventable through routine practice.
We're not there yet.
But every year, we're noticeably closer.
